subject: Chemistry
title: Taxol: Cancer Drug From a Tree
description: A micro-lesson that makes Taxol: Cancer Drug From a Tree usable.

hook.text: Taxol, one of the most effective cancer drugs ever, comes from the bark of the Pacific yew tree—a slow-growing endangered species.

buildup.text: In 1962, the National Cancer Institute collected bark from a yew tree in Washington State. The extract killed cancer cells.

discovery.text: Taxol stabilizes microtubules inside cells, freezing the machinery needed for cell division. Cancer cells can't split and die.

twist.text: Making one dose required killing six 100-year-old trees. Environmentalists and cancer patients were in direct conflict.

climax.text: Chemists learned to semi-synthesize Taxol from yew needles—no tree killing needed. It now treats breast, ovarian, and lung cancer.

punchline.text: A tree bark that freezes cell division. Nature's chemistry, humanity's cure.

quiz.question: How does Taxol kill cancer cells?
options: It stabilizes microtubules, preventing cells from dividing
It dissolves cancer cell membranes on contact
It blocks blood supply to tumors by collapsing blood vessels
